item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image used in the article comes from here, with no indication that it has been released under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: STAFF POST- OPEN Crosslink Proposal.
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show reasoning
Hide Reasoning

The following crosslink is being proposed due to both anomalies being food related, and being produced by Wondertainment.

Editing this:

Personnel wishing to conduct tests involving SCP-1842 should submit the Experiment Request form to the current researcher staffed with testing the object, or the Director of Site-118's Gamma Wing.

To this:

Personnel wishing to conduct tests involving SCP-1842 should submit the Experiment Request form to the current researcher staffed with testing the object, or the Director of Site-118's Gamma Wing.

SCP-1842-2 should not be used to create cylindrical shapes resembling soup cans due to the object's tendency to produce SCP-2057 instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"my Super Special Putty-Mix,™"

We aren't supposed to know if Dr. Wondertainment™ is an organization, an individual, or something else entirely. Try to use vague, non personal language when writing notes from Dr. Wondertainment™ (I would replace "my" with "the" or "a").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This last test with the foie gras and tarte tatin made me hungry (I love those two dishes). Not that that was needed for my upvote, as I like SCPs that are (mostly) harmless, but still strange while not overly useful (the zero nutritional value is what prevents this to become a fully functional Star Trek replicator).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really like this one. I don't know how much it has been edited since the previous comments, but I don't have the problems that some others have with it. The only issue I have is that it doesn't make sense why they freaked out during test 7 when test 6 just proved it would only make food. Other than that, I'm 100% on-board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just comes off as overly formulaic. Nothing really caught my interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something identified only as "a brown cube" could be plastic for all we know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you've made it. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe the D-class that tested this eat guns?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand that rationale, but there are certainly many other things that are not considered "food" by various groups that could have been tested just as easily. Jumping to cannibalism just seems ooky for the sake of ookiness. I find it difficult to imagine that during a meeting designing test protocols, every Foundation researcher agreed human was vitally important to test. Twice.

Again, I enjoy the concept, and Wondertainment is difficult to do well. No downvoting here, just can't go into upvote territory personally. Doesn't seem like you need mine though, as the article's doing quite well without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cubes of meat? A brownie? A slice of cake? Lots of foods are brown cubes. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not necessarily the "gross-out" factor of a human arm, but more of the fact that it's not considered a food by most people on the planet. Thus, it gives a useful means of testing out the viability of "food."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except "a brown cube" isn't food. (Unless you're playing Risk of Rain.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tripped slightly over the cannibalism bits. How large is this thing that it can accommodate an arm? Why are we so curious about "human steak" that we run multiple tests trying to get it to replicate cooked person? I like gruesome when called for, but it's out of sync here, in my opinion.

I like the underlying concept, and the exploration of how it interacts with a user's perception, but the needless long piggery is distracting. No vote for the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's sort of addressed in the second test, involving the block that tastes of pasta. Obviously, pasta doesn't come in bricks, but the object takes the taste of what is imagined, not necessarily shaped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if you shape one food but think of another food? (No, not the same as the last test.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. This is cute, but, especially as Wondertainment skips go, it's not overly interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only small detail I would point. When a SCP includes a non-replenishing ammount of functional 'stuff' (in this case, -2 will run out at some point if they keep testing) it's usually mentioned as a reason not to test, or at least a more detailed ammount of how much is left is pointed to avoid testers sneaking bits of it. At least is what I noticed on the oooold school SCPs ^^

Other than that, do like, and fits my Wondertainment headcanon~ have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hahah, my bad. I'm too used to lurking.
Take the first ever +1 I've given [I think].
*really needs to get round to upvoting stuff I like*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, my bad. Didn't get it. I assume this is a neutral vote then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any particular thoughts on what to add/change in order to reach that oh so elusive upvote threshold?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh yes, I understand that. That was half my point with the last paragraph.
The other half was just for the sake of a joke. My bad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, this is continuing to improve. The final test is good. It addresses a clever issue that I hadn't thought of. I would strongly recommend you put both footnotes into the main body of the article, because the information they contain is significant and not at all parenthetical. Turns out I actually wasn't downvoting this after all, but you're real close to an upvote.

Good job with the continued improvement!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing about Wondertainment is that all of the toys made by Wondertainment are toys, but designed by someone who doesn't really understand humans that well. They often harm humans accidentally, but in the long term, that's not what they're designed for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know it's not actually Play-Doh, and I'm not at all recommending changing it.
BUT:

Play-Doh and most similar substances are more often than not non-toxic, due to the creators being wonderfully responsible people who know their product will be played with by small children.

Also, knowing this is a Wondertainment deal, I fully realise that my previous statement regard wonderfully responsible people is a moot point. Just saying, though. Not a criticism or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, sorry, this still doesn't work for me. You've fixed some things, but there's still awkward phrasing. Describing the non-toxicity of -3:

However, instances of SCP-1842-3 possesses no nutritional value, and should not be consumed.

However, once SCP-1842-2 is converted into SCP-1842-3, it is no longer toxic. However, it still retains no nutritional value.

These shouldn't both be there. I'd rearrange things to have the paragraph describing -2's toxic effects before you get into the main anomaly. It is burying the lede, but it prevents you from bouncing back and forth so much between topics. You also way, way overuse the word "however".

The changes to the test log are okay, but there's a lot of logical issues with the trebuchet. You establish with the arm that it only produces stuff that the user perceives to be food, and though cannibal cults may exist, I don't think trebuchetvorous cults do. Plus, considering the physics of a trebuchet, a functional one made out of foodstuffs as described would also be impossible. For that matter, making one out of play-dough that doesn't fall apart would be quite a feat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the way that I imagined this when writing it was that it was sort of like an Easy-Bake Oven meets Play-doh. The kid takes the material, makes some food, and pops it into the microwave. Ding, out pops your analogous representation of food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt the same way about the "lead clay" angle while in chat, but I didn't bring it up because I wasn't sure what to replace it with in terms of an angle.

I don't know… maybe the machine takes anything as input and produces indulgent food with the nutritional value of whatever you put in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took out the functional gun component, and replaced it with a little bit of a twist instead. Any other thoughts on how to change the bad components of the SCP Classic vibe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I took out a good number of the SCP-1842's, and replaced them with various pronouns. Addressed the rest of the issues too. You weren't the only one who thought -3 shouldn't be toxic, but it wasn't a point that a lot of people on chat raised (until now.)

I sort of put a spin on the last test. Hopefully it makes a little more sense now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a distinct SCP Classic vibe to it, right down to the white background product photo. Not to say that's a bad thing.

This isn't so bad that I'd spit it out, but it's not so good that I'd go back for seconds. Overall, I found it to be a little on the bland side. The final test log was amusing, but the fact that it produced a gun at all contradicts the machine's established limitations. +-0 Nopevote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, there's a number of writing problems with this. First off, this is number soup (soup would be interesting to try with this, actually). This line:

This also applies to the ingestion of SCP-1842-3.

Seems to imply the resulting foods are actively toxic, rather than just being nutrition-free. Which strikes me as implausibly nonfunctional. Wondertainment doesn't release stuff that would harm children if used as directed. I can believe the -2 as being harmful if swallowed, though not anomalously so, but it does not match the universe to have -3 be so. It's unclear how exactly the last test worked, if it can only produce food items.

Tests have indicated that the output of SCP-1842-2

This line reads as off. -2 isn't something that has output.

The Foundation Toxicology lab has confirmed

This is phrased poorly. Why not "Toxicology tests show that…"?

This is a solid premise, but the execution is flawed, and it never goes anywhere. Downvoting for now. If you can fix these issues, I'd neutral vote, and if you can have the article go a more sensible and interesting direction than the gun test, I'd probably upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said before, this is wonderful, even if it obliterates my inner childs hopes and dreams.

brb eating play-doh +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I conclude that this skip is delicious!

Also horribly toxic

Oh, the food isn't toxic anymore. In that case, I WANT THIS MICROWAVE. No nutritional value means I can gorge myself on the most disgustingly delicious foods in the world and not become spherical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god. Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's got a ton of Xs everywhere.

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to all the folks in chat who helped me out with this, especially CryogenChaos, Silberescher, Tuomey, Voct, and assorted other folk.

The photo is from a picture of a Wonderworld Toy Neo Microwave from Wonderworld Toys, a company that has not updated their website in three years, and which I believe is no longer active.
